groups.context_description: Term names justice as the defining inner character of God — the disposition of right dealing that flows from who God is
Term names justice as the inner moral orientation of the person — the disposition to seek, do, and love what is right in relation to others
Term names the inner cry for justice — the longing of the person denied their right, reaching toward God for vindication
Term names divine judgment as the event before which the inner person stands in appeal — the plea-context of God's judicial acts
Term describes condemnation as the verdict passed on the inner person on the basis of their inner state — unbelief, doubt, or moral failure becoming the grounds of condemnation
Term names the freedom from condemnation — the security of the inner person before God when condemnation is withheld or overcome
Term names the condition of the inner person whose own beliefs or conduct become the instrument of their condemnation — the conscience turned against the self
Term names God's judicial acts as the consequence flowing from inner moral failure — the execution of judgment that reflects the inner state and conduct of those judged
Term names condemnation as an inner state produced by the law's verdict — the weight of moral judgment falling on the conscience, contrasted with the freedom of righteousness
Term names judgment as the coming event that orients and shapes the inner life — the fearful expectation of divine verdict that calls for moral accountability and reverence
Term names culpable spiritual ignorance — the inner condition of not knowing God's character, ways, or will, which becomes the basis of moral failure or self-deception
Term names the divine act of foreknowing as the ground of election and identity — the prior knowing of persons by God that constitutes their inner standing and destiny
Term names divine foreknowledge as the sovereign inner ground of God's redemptive purpose — the prior knowing from which election and redemption flow
Term names the act of foreseeing — the inner capacity of prophetic or divine perception that reads the future and shapes present understanding
Term names the act of inner self-awareness or conscience — the person's own consciousness of their moral state, which nonetheless remains subject to God's higher judgment
Term names a conscious inner awareness — moral complicity or cognitive recognition — that informs volitional response
groups.notes: Anchored — dimension analytically unambiguous. C01 anchor session 2026-04-06.

groups.context_description: Term names the inward parts as the seat of the innermost self — the deepest layer of the person's inner life that God searches, tests, and knows
Term names the inward parts as the seat of inner experience — the deepest place in the person where joy, pain, instruction, and affliction are felt
Eagerness as the inner disposition of forward-leaning readiness toward God and good
Inner courage/cheerfulness grounded in faith amid crisis
Cheerfulness as the inner-being ground of praise
The willing spirit — inner eagerness toward God in tension with fleshly weakness
Eagerness as the inner motivation for mission
Cheerful inner composure and confidence in adversarial or crisis contexts
The inner state of discouragement — loss of heart produced by provocation
Eager willing service — inner orientation of freedom and genuine care contrasted with compulsion and greed
Covetousness as the insatiable inner orientation toward possessing more
Covetousness as idolatry — the ultimate inner-being misalignment
Term names the wilful refusal to hear — the inner orientation of closed attention or contempt expressed through the repeated failure to receive the voice of the community

Term names disobedience as the inner condition and defining orientation of the person before God — the state of being characterised by active resistance or refusal; the inner disposition from which the wrath of God follows
Term names disobedience as the inner failure that excludes from rest — the disposition of the heart that prevents entry into what God offers; disobedience as spiritual self-exclusion

Term names disobedience as the defining inner act with moral and covenantal consequence — the primal refusal that constituted the human condition; the act or disposition of disobedience that draws retribution and demands correction

Term names the snatching that threatens or violates the inner being — the evil one taking the word from the heart, the wolf scattering the sheep, and the divine security that makes such snatching impossible

Term names the divine catching away of the person — eschatological rapture, Spirit transportation, and protective snatching to God — acts in which the inner person is taken into the divine presence or safety

Term names greed as an inner-being condition of the person — the inner fullness of rapacious desire that contradicts external religious propriety, and whose opposite is the inner freedom that accepts loss with joy

Term names the greedy person as a moral-character type — the inner disposition of insatiable acquisitive desire that constitutes idolatry, excludes from the kingdom, and disqualifies from community in Christ
Term names the personal confession of sin before God — the inner turning point at which the sinner names their sin, takes ownership, and turns toward God in acknowledgement
Term names sin as the sustained inner-being orientation away from God — the pattern of covenantal unfaithfulness characterising individuals and generations who walk in idolatry
Term names the moral wisdom about sin — the universal reality that no one is without sin, the consequences sin brings, and the inner posture that safeguards against it
Term names sin as the offence and burden carried by persons and peoples before God — the sin confessed, forgiven, covered, borne, and purged through divine grace
Term names sin as the named offence confessed to and before God — the great sin acknowledged and the prayer for covering and forgiveness that follows
Term names iniquity as the inner-being condition of moral debt before God — the accumulated perversion of the human heart that God marks, bears, forgives, and will ultimately remember no more
Term names guiltlessness — the inner moral condition of innocence recognised and vindicated by God, whose condemnation reveals the inner failure of those who judge
Term names guilt as the inner-being weight of iniquity — the moral stain that accumulates, is confessed and borne, and can be removed by God alone
Term names the experienced weight of punishment as felt from within — the inner burden of consequence for wrongdoing, whether already borne or anticipated
Term names the inward act of perverting and committing iniquity — the moral distortion of the inner being that is confessed, punished, and turned from in repentance
Term names the sinner as the one Jesus came to call — the person whose inner condition of sinfulness is both their social designation and the ground of divine mercy toward them
Term names the sin committed before Paul's coming — the prior inner-being condition of unrepented sin that Paul mourns over and addresses
Term names sin as the offence before God that is confessed, forgiven, and covered — the inner-being condition addressed through repentance, intercession, and divine cleansing

Term names the sin offering as the divinely instituted cultic mechanism through which sin is atoned for — the act by which the inner-being condition of guilt is addressed before God through sacrifice and priestly mediation
Term names the sinner as a moral-character type whose inner orientation persistently transgresses against God — contrasted with the righteous in the community of God
Term names the punishment that corresponds to sin — the divine consequence that falls on those who persist in sin, which itself becomes a form of chastisement

Term names the moral ground of a person's standing — the charge, reason, or basis for guilt or innocence, including the declared innocence of Christ and the inner conviction that grounds Paul's suffering without shame
Term names the causal ground of guilt or salvation — the found absence of guilt in Christ and his being the source of eternal salvation
Term names the conscience as inner moral witness — it bears testimony, accuses, or excuses the person regarding their conduct before God and others
Term names the conscience as morally vulnerable — susceptible to defilement, weakening through wrong associations, or cauterisation by persistent sin
Term names the conscience as the interior moral foundation of godly living — holding faith and conduct with a clear or good conscience
Term names the conscience as the object of Christ's redemptive purification — what the old sacrificial system could not cleanse, his blood can
Term names the inner-being event of incurring and realising guilt — the moment when a person becomes guilty before God and the recognition of that guilt that precedes confession and atonement
Term names the bearing of guilt and its communal consequences — the guilt that brings judgment on individuals and nations who persist in sin, including inner acknowledgement and confession

Term names the guilt offering as the divinely instituted response to guilt — the atonement mechanism that addresses the inner-being condition of guilt, supremely fulfilled in the Servant's soul as the guilt offering
Term names the state of being guilty — the inner recognition of guilt named by the person themselves, including the communal conscience that speaks before God
Term names guiltiness as the accumulated inner-spiritual condition of a person or community before God — the weight of guilt that accumulates, is confessed, and is addressed through atonement
Term names sin as the guilt and offence before God that requires forgiveness — the relational breach that Christ came to address, that is confessed, atoned for, and forgiven through his blood

Term names sin as an indwelling power that rules, enslaves, and deceives the inner being — the force that reigns in the mortal body, dwells within the person, and wages war against the law of the mind
Term names sin as the universal human condition and objective state of all persons apart from Christ — the condition that defines, condemns, and is overcome only by the righteousness of God
Term names the physical structure of justice — the hall where right judgment is pronounced, embodying the inner commitment to justice in institutional form
Term names the hall of royal authority — the place where sovereign power and its inner orientation toward justice are made manifest
Term characterises the person as morally righteous — the inner quality of justice and uprightness that defines the person who stands rightly before God and others
Term characterises God or Christ as just and righteous — the divine inner attribute of perfect moral justice
Term names the divine act of justification — the declaration that transforms the person's inner moral standing before God, received through faith
Term characterises the person or action as morally unjust — the inner condition of unrighteousness that stands opposed to God and his standards
Term names the quality of acting or suffering in a morally right manner — the adverbial dimension of inner moral alignment expressed in conduct
Term names the adversary in legal or spiritual contest — the opposition that calls forth inner urgency, persistence, or vigilance
Term names the inner act of moral condemnation — the judgment that pronounces another guilty and the divine prohibition of such judgment by the unmerciful
Term names the act or condition of moral wrongdoing — the inner moral deficit that constitutes crime before God and human courts
Term names justification as the act that restores right standing before God — the inner transformation of moral status through Christ's work
Term names the human judge or ruler in the context of the inner act of rejecting divinely appointed authority
Term names the just or righteous character of a consequence or penalty — the inner moral logic of appropriate retribution
Term names the quality of enduring unjust suffering — the inner disposition of patient endurance before God when wronged
Term names the righteous judgment of God — the divine standard of just assessment that confronts the hard-hearted inner condition
Term names the condition of moral accountability before God — the inner standing of being answerable to divine judgment
Term names the law or decree as the framework within which inner moral and religious commitment is tested — the external standard that reveals the inner orientation of the person
Term names the divine act of judging — God's inner determination to vindicate his people, execute justice among the nations, and rule with righteousness
Term names the human act of judging on behalf of the poor — the inner moral obligation to give right judgment to the vulnerable
Term names the judgment that determines the outcome for powers and persons — the authoritative verdict of God or the court that shapes inner destiny
Term names God as the divine judge — the one whose inner justice and attentiveness to the wronged defines the nature of true judgment
Term names the inner act of perceiving or approving something as right — the moral/personal assessment that something accords with what is right and true
Term names the quality of moral uprightness — the straight inner path and conduct that reflects alignment with God's ways
Term names the quality of moral and spiritual uprightness — the inner straightness of the person who walks rightly before God and detests what perverts justice
Term names the avenging character of divine authority — the inner justice that punishes wrongdoing and protects the wronged
Term names the perversion of justice — the inner moral distortion of a people that has abandoned divine standards and denies God's seeing
Soul's orientation toward God's mishpat — longing, delight, fear, praise, hope
Moral-spiritual orientation of the heart — faithfulness, rebellion, or Spirit-enabled transformation
Inner moral standard for human judgement — impartiality, righteousness, God-accountability
Judicial arena where inner life meets divine justice — humility, complaint, vindication-hope
Justice as spirit-given capacity for inner discernment and righteous action
Judging as requiring inner moral discernment — righteousness, impartiality, God-accountability in the judge
Inner appeal to God as judge — cry for divine vindication, justice, or moral verdict
Divine judgement engaging inner moral condition — judged according to ways, wickedness, spiritual orientation
God as righteous judge whose inner character is expressed through just acts — indignation, equity, faithfulness
Righteousness as defining inner quality of the person — moral character, integrity, standing before God
Righteousness as attribute of God — character expressed in just acts, judgements, steadfast faithfulness
Righteousness as inner orientation pursued and cultivated — active seeking, learning, walking in righteousness
Righteousness as moral basis for just governance — inner character required of kings, judges, and rulers
Term names the cause or right of the person — the just claim that calls for inner moral commitment from the judge and from God
Term names divine and judicial verdict — the authoritative inner determination of guilt or innocence pronounced by God or the court
Term names the inner cry and petition for divine justice — the persistent appeal of the wronged person to God for vindication
Term names the execution of divine vengeance and justice — God's inner determination to right wrongs and vindicate the oppressed
Term names the divine vengeance and justice that arises from God's inner righteousness in response to evil — and the human cry for such justice
Inner moral standing before God — being righteous, accounted righteous, or incapacity to be righteous
Judicial act of vindicating the righteous or condemning the wicked — requiring inner moral discernment
Term names the just punishment or condemnation that follows moral transgression — the consequence of the inner condition of wickedness before divine justice
Term names the divine righteousness revealed and imputed — the righteousness of God that becomes the ground of the person's standing before him
Term names human righteousness as an inner quality and lived conduct — the moral character and orientation of the person who lives rightly before God
Term names righteousness obtained and pursued by faith — the inner orientation of seeking righteousness through trust in God rather than works
Term names the act of turning or transferring as inner spiritual change — deserting truth, perverting grace, or the institutional change that reshapes the inner-relational pathway to God

Term names the unchangeable character of God's purpose and truthfulness as the foundation for inner encouragement and hope — the immutability of divine commitment produces assurance in the inner person

Term names the irrevocable quality of God's gifts and calling (grounding identity) and the quality of genuine repentance that produces no regret — immutability as a characteristic of both divine commitment and authentic inner transformation
Term names repentance as the inner-being transformation — the godly grief that leads to reorientation of life toward God, given as a divine gift and leading to forgiveness and life
groups.notes: Anchored — dimension analytically unambiguous. C01 anchor session 2026-04-06.

groups.context_description: Term names the withholding of divine compassion — the inner-relational disposition of God toward a people under judgment, where compassion is deliberately hidden
Term names the human act of comforting — bringing relief to inner grief through presence, words, or care
groups.notes: Anchored — dimension analytically unambiguous. C01 anchor session 2026-04-06.

groups.context_description: Term names divine comfort — God actively bringing relief and restoration to the inner grief of his people
groups.notes: Anchored — dimension analytically unambiguous. C01 anchor session 2026-04-06.

groups.context_description: Term names human relenting — the inner change of heart toward repentance before God or reversal of a previous disposition
groups.notes: Anchored — dimension analytically unambiguous. C01 anchor session 2026-04-06.

groups.context_description: Term names divine relenting — God's inner-being disposition changing in response to human repentance or intercession, turning from intended judgment
groups.notes: Anchored — dimension analytically unambiguous. C01 anchor session 2026-04-06.

groups.context_description: Term names repentance as the inner-being act of turning — the complete reorientation of the person from sin toward God, called for by prophets, apostles, and Christ himself
groups.notes: Anchored — dimension analytically unambiguous. C01 anchor session 2026-04-06.

groups.context_description: Term names the inner reversal of a previous decision — the change of mind or feeling of regret that follows a prior action or stance
groups.notes: Anchored — dimension analytically unambiguous. C01 anchor session 2026-04-06.

groups.context_description: Term names the inner act of recalling and turning to mind — deliberate inner movement of memory and reflection that leads to repentance, discernment, or hope
Term names repentance as the inner act of turning toward God — the deliberate inner reorientation of the whole person from evil ways back to covenantal faithfulness, with all the heart and soul

Term names God's relenting as a divine inner-relational response to human repentance — God turning from judgment when his people turn, expressing the inner disposition of divine compassion and the withholding of wrath
